generated_from: live scan of every test_cases.function_args/function_expected value across the full corpus, cross-checked against contracts.py TypeSpec kind == "integer" mappings per language (compiled_adapters.py _c_type/_rust_type/_java_type/_csharp_type/_cpp_type), and the real ledger (2026-07-07)
finding: 10 problems have real 40-case corpus values exceeding int64 range (up to 1023 decimal digits), but their function_contract declares return/parameter type kind="integer" with no arbitrary-precision distinction in the TypeSpec schema.

affected_problems.fibonacci-dp: fibonacci-dp(4892) = a 1023-digit integer (stress case alongside small n=0..40 cases)
affected_problems.karatsuba: large-integer multiplication -- inherently produces huge products by design
affected_problems.catalan-number: catalan(n) for large n grows super-exponentially
affected_problems.decode-ways: count of decodings can grow combinatorially for long digit strings
affected_problems.unique-paths: large grid dimensions -- binomial coefficient growth
affected_problems.combination-sum-iv-count: combinatorial count, can exceed int64 for large targets
affected_problems.evaluate-reverse-polish-notation: unbounded intermediate products in the expression
affected_problems.sum-root-to-leaf-numbers: deep trees produce large concatenated-digit sums

why_this_matters: Python's `int` is natively arbitrary-precision, so all 10 problems are already genuinely Level-6 verified for Python (see docs/atlascode-function-mode-python-runtime-verification.json / the legacy-oracle closure) using the exact real 1023-digit test case, not a truncated one. But the SAME 40-case corpus is meant to be reused verbatim across every language (mission's own no-duplicate-corpus rule) -- so any future correct reference solution for these 10 problems in C, C++, Rust, Java, C#, Go, Kotlin, Scala, Swift, PHP, Perl, R, Ruby will FAIL the large-value cases unless it uses that language's real arbitrary-precision facility (Java/C# BigInteger, PHP GMP/bcmath, Perl Math::BigInt, Ruby's native bignum promotion, Python-like), NOT the fixed-width native int the current contract-to-language type mapping emits.

current_type_mapping_is_fixed_width.c: _c_type("integer") == "int" (32-bit, max ~2.1e9) -- cannot represent a 1023-digit value at all; C has no standard-library bigint
current_type_mapping_is_fixed_width.rust: _rust_type("integer") == "i32" -- same problem; would need num-bigint crate or a hand-rolled bignum
current_type_mapping_is_fixed_width.cpp/java/csharp/go/kotlin/scala/swift: similarly fixed-width (int/long/int64) per their respective _*_type functions -- not yet audited value-by-value in this pass, but the same TypeSpec kind="integer" is used uniformly, so the same gap applies wherever the language's native int/long is emitted

trapping-rain-water.evidence: real corpus stress case expects 24932455072 (~2.49e10), confirmed by a genuine reference-solution failure in java/cpp/csharp/c/rust (all correctly computed -837348704, the exact int32-overflow wraparound of the true value) during this session's array-cluster batch -- not a bug in the reference algorithm, a real fixed-width-return-type ceiling.
trapping-rain-water.why_js_ts_perl_work: JS/TS numbers are float64 (exact integers up to 2^53); Perl scalars auto-promote to double/bignum on overflow -- neither is fixed-width 32-bit the way C/C++/C#/Java/Rust's `int`/`i32` return type is.
trapping-rain-water.excluded_this_session_from: javascript/typescript/java/cpp/csharp/perl/c/rust array-cluster batch (java/cpp/csharp/c/rust deliberately left unattempted for this one problem after the real failure was confirmed, not silently skipped)

reverse-bits.evidence: corpus includes reverse_bits(1) = 2147483648 (2^31), one more than INT32_MAX (2147483647) -- cannot be represented in a signed 32-bit return type at all, found by inspection before attempting (not run against other languages yet, deliberately deferred from the bit-misc batch this session)

combination-sum-count.evidence: real corpus stress case (30 candidates, target=490) expects 398814034740 (~4e11), confirmed by a genuine reference-solution failure in java/cpp/csharp/c/rust (all silently wrapped/truncated) during the mega-cluster-4 batch -- javascript/typescript (float64) and perl (auto-promoting scalars) passed correctly.
combination-sum-count.excluded_this_session_from: java/cpp/csharp/c/rust mega-cluster-4 batch -- deliberately left unattempted for this one problem after the real failure was confirmed, not silently skipped

recommendation: Do NOT silently weaken these 10 problems' test corpora to remove the large-value stress cases just to make other languages pass (would violate the no-duplicate/no-weakened-corpus rule and hide a real precision bug). Do NOT attempt naive fixed-width-int reference solutions for these 10 in other languages -- they will genuinely and correctly fail on the large cases, which is CORRECT judge behavior, not a bug to route around. The real fix is future, separate work: either (a) add a genuine arbitrary-precision TypeSpec kind with real per-language bigint support, or (b) mark these 10 problems ARCHITECTURAL_LIMITATION for non-Python languages until that work lands. Excluded from the current sort-family and any other broad-scaling batch in this session for exactly this reason.
status: documented, not fixed -- flagged as a real blocker for full non-Python Function Mode coverage of these 10 problems specifically, not the other 206
